data.title: Brandywine Realty Trust: Some Bright Spots Emerging In Office Market, Finally
data.description:
To wrap up quite an interesting month of March for stocks and financial markets, and 11 years since I first worked in an office in downtown Austin, Texas, in the spring of 2015, today's article explores an office REIT with exposure both to the Austin and Philadelphia office markets, among other metro areas, and that is Brandywine Realty Trust (BDN).

The stock got my attention as it was trading under $3/share as of Monday, with a dividend yield topping 12%, and its next earnings results being in late April, so this is a great time to preview the stock ahead of that earnings call.

According to BDN's Seeking Alpha profile, they "own, develop, lease, and manage an urban, town center, and transit-oriented portfolio comprising 120 properties and 20.0 million square feet."

My research covers 8 rating categories, with a goal of giving BDN a holistic rating score after considering both fundamental and technical analysis.

Thesis Summary

Based on my research, for my initial coverage today of BDN, I called this stock a hold.

There are some bright spots for BDN and office property, as new leases were achieved in FY25 and portfolio expansion as well, but several other factors weigh down on this stock and hurt any bullish view for now, particularly its technical trends, high D/E, and recent downgrade from S&P Global, as well as weak competitive positioning within a similar peer group.

The above worksheet shows how each factor contributed to the overall score. Keep reading to learn more about how each of these topics impacted this REIT.

Macro & Sector Trends

To kick off today's discussion, from a wider angle of understanding the macroeconomic and sector environment, I gave the stock a neutral/hold score here.

Considering that this REIT's portfolio has heavy exposure to two markets, Austin and metro Philadelphia, we should consider some data showing office vacancy rates being higher there than in some US metros.

For instance, according to Commercial Cafe, the Feb. 2026 office vacancy rate in Austin was 24.6%, Philadelphia was at 18.2%, and Washington, DC, was at 19.5%, while Florida metros were much lower (Miami being the lowest at 12.8% and Tampa at 14.3%).

That could point to much lower demand or tenant retention in BDN's key markets of Austin and Philadelphia, thereby presenting greater downside risk.

Nationwide, though, it seems there is some positive data pointing to a rebound in absorption since the pandemic years. For instance, citing data from Newmark, CoStar reported "about 9.5MM square feet of positive absorption was reported throughout the final quarter of 2025 alone, Newmark found, a stark comparison to the negative 30MM square feet throughout the same time in 2020."

In addition, there seems to be a supply/demand imbalance in the office niche. The article went on to say, "a number of landlords - including Cousins, BXP, and Vornado Realty Trust - say the leasing momentum will only build as construction hits all-time lows and the demand for premium space continues to climb."

So, that could provide some upside for existing office REITs like BDN and others if a low amount of new supply continues impacting the market yet demand rises, and in particular, what could help BDN is what appears to be a focus on "premium" office property. For instance, a great example is its property in Northern Virginia/DC Metro that is pitched as a new development that "offers market-leading amenities to welcome forward-thinking tenants."

Although this sector faced headwinds from the emergence of remote work in the wake of the pandemic, recently some data shows it also has seen a rebound as companies started requiring a return to the office, or a hybrid model, and at least a growing trend towards that direction, which could start driving better upside for the office property sector in the near future.

According to Forbes in Dec 2025, there is an increasing trend in return-to-work mandates. The article cited Frank Weishaupt, CEO of Owl Labs, who said that "companies are steadily calling employees back to the office, and among hybrid workers the required in-office days keep climbing, with 34% now going into the office four days a week, up from just 23% in 2023."

Hence, I believe this sector is in a balancing act between growing demand and low supply on the one hand, but also some metros performing better than others. With two metros being alike and this particular REIT being very regionally concentrated rather than nationwide, I am more inclined to be neutral on it than bullish at this time.

Competitive Positioning

Moving ahead, I considered the competitive positioning of BDN vs. 3 peers I picked, and based on the results, I gave this section a sell rating.

The peers picked were office REITs within a similar market cap range as BDN: Hudson Pacific Properties (HPP), Piedmont Realty Trust (PDM), and Peakstone Realty Trust (PKST).

From peer data, BDN saw weaker top-line figures than some peers, with a 5-year revenue CAGR at -3.5%, putting it 3rd in this peer group, along with an ROE of -19.4%, the worst in this peer group, pointing to a struggle to generate a positive return on equity.

In terms of share price performance vs. a major benchmark like the S&P 500, in the hopes that investors can achieve "alpha," this stock did not quite cut it with a 10-year negative price return of -59.5%, well behind the S&P 500, and putting it 3rd in this peer group. The momentum from investors simply was not there, and I can understand why, considering the huge pandemic impact on the office sector but also perhaps some lingering uncertainty versus some other types of REITs. Notice that none of these peers even came close to the return of the S&P 500.

In terms of qualitative factors, BDN has a portfolio geographic exposure that is limited (heavy concentration in the PA area, Austin, and some in the DC area, with no large nationwide or global exposure).

Consider that data in its recent investor deck (pg. 2) shows that 76% of NOI (net operating income) is exposed to the greater Philadelphia area (metro and PA suburbs), which I think is quite a lot of regional exposure:

So, not only did this REIT underperform office REITs of similar size historically, but it also has a very geographically concentrated portfolio, moving the risk needle up for me in the near term as well, and so I am compelled to be bearish in this section.

Company Growth Factors

Next, I looked into company-specific factors that could impact growth, and in this section I gave the stock a neutral rating.

Here are the key points I discovered.

To start with, from Seeking Alpha data, there is a lack of confidence in near-term top-line growth, with the forward revenue growth at -1.59%.

Second, in an equity REIT, I usually look for portfolio expansion or optimization. When it comes to that, Q4 and FY25 results (pg 1) had some bright spots in terms of new leases signed, with the company saying, "for full year 2025, we signed 790,000 square feet in our wholly owned portfolio."

Further, from the same results (pg. 2), portfolio growth happened through acquisition. The REIT said, "we acquired our partner's preferred equity interest in 3151 Market Street, a 417,000-square-foot office/life science building located in Philadelphia, and acquired our partner's preferred equity interest in 3025 JFK, located in Philadelphia."

So, both of the above factors could drive future upside through future rental income flows, which could ultimately help drive the top line. What I love about equity REITs is that leases are typically long-term, unlike selling a single product one time, so there can be a more predictive view of future cash flows.

However, there were still some headwinds in terms of tenant retention and absorption metrics. In those same quarterly results, the company stated:

We achieved a 54% tenant retention ratio in our core portfolio with negative net absorption of (57,000) square feet during the fourth quarter of 2025 which includes 74,000 square feet of negative absorption due to tenants exercising early termination rights within their leases.

Nevertheless, this REIT has been active with its development pipeline, with 3 projects in Austin and Pennsylvania that I would highlight and what appears to be mixed use (office, parking, residential), according to their investor deck (pg. 5).

This mixed-use approach could certainly add to portfolio diversification rather than being a pure-play office REIT.

Personally, as someone who rode the wave of the Austin, Texas, population growth and tech/business growth from 2015 to 2020 and then saw the impact of the global pandemic after that, when streets quickly became a ghost town and offices were shuttered, I tend to have a mixed view when it comes to the Austin office market, at least when it comes to traditional office space.

For instance, I tend to see the value in co-working/flexible office space over the more traditional variety. In fact, since launching my equities research firm, I have often used co-working spaces rather than a traditional office.

So, I think BDN will have the challenge of proving to investors and analysts in subsequent quarterly results that it can improve its tenant retention rates while also continuing to grow new leases. In the meantime, I am neutral in this category.

Earnings Outlook

Moving on next to the topic of earnings, after considering margins, FFO/AFFO growth, as well as forward projections, I am neutral in this section too, giving the stock a hold rating here too.

The EBITDA margin vs. peers is a great comparison tool for an equity REIT, since it strips away accounting noise from depreciation. In this case, BDN is the 2nd lowest in the group, with an EBITDA margin of 53%, which is respectable and not a major outlier but also is easily beat by 2 peers.

Further, since this is a REIT, I like to track FFO/AFFO growth trends, and from Seeking Alpha growth data, there is not much to boast about, with this REIT achieving an FFO 5-year CAGR of -17.8% and an AFFO 5-year CAGR of -17%.

The bright spot is not so much historical performance but potentially the future, with the analyst consensus showing a positive FFO growth forecast this year and next, indicating that there is starting to be greater confidence in this REIT looking ahead. That could support investors taking the long view on this stock and being willing to eat the short-term headwinds for now.

The company's own guidance for FY26, according to Q4 results (pg. 3), was an FFO of $0.51-$0.59/share, which is roughly in line with $0.52/share for the year ending Dec. 2025, shown on the income statement.

Hence, my view is neutral in this section too, and this is based on both the weaker EBITDA margin vs. peers and the negative historic FFO growth, but also the more positive future outlook, which this REIT will have to prove it can execute.

Balance Sheet Risk

When considering balance sheet risk, I was actually more bearish than in the prior few sections and gave this section a sell rating. Here is why.

First, the D/E vs. peers is the highest in this peer group at 3.27, and it is also showing a rising trend, which indicates more reliance on leverage than these other office REITs.

That certainly moves the risk needle up, particularly in a REIT niche that has been struggling with tenant retention in some metro areas.

Next, a major downside exists for this stock since the S&P Global downgrade to BB- in March 2026, along with a negative outlook. I think many investors and analysts will look rather unfavorably on this downgrade. In their rating note, S&P said, in part, "our downgrade reflects risk related to BDN's short weighted-average debt maturity."

In addition, I considered asset risk exposure on the asset side of the balance sheet, and like I mentioned earlier, this REIT has heavy concentration in a few geographies: the Philadelphia area, the Austin area, and a few others like the DC area. However, the majority of NOI is driven by the Philadelphia/PA area, so that's a huge geographic exposure.

Thus, there are too many negatives in this section weighing down on this stock, and so I am bearish this time.

Dividend Outlook

When it comes to dividends, I had more of a neutral view, and here is why.

I considered the yield, growth, and coverage, and here is what I found.

The dividend yield of 12.96% is well above the sector average and could appeal to high-yield investors if yield were the only factor. However, as an experienced analyst on this platform, I would like to point out additional factors like growth and safety. The fact is, this is not the only high-yield REIT, as several mortgage REITs are yielding above 12%. For instance, the yield on AGNC (AGNC) is above 14% as of this week.

The weak spot for BDN is a proven dividend growth over time and through many shocks, since they achieved a negative 10-year div CAGR of -2.6%.

Where this REIT looks much better is not in proven growth but in terms of dividend safety and AFFO coverage. For instance, their quarterly dividend is $0.08/share, and income statement data shows AFFO/share in the range of $0.14 - $0.16 in FY25, implying AFFO coverage of 1.75x - 2x, so it seems to have adequate dividend coverage if going by those figures, as well as its FFO projection for FY26 that I mentioned in the section on earnings.

Also, with the analyst consensus showing an FFO YoY growth of 6-9% this year and next, meeting or beating that forecast should further provide dividend support, and that is something I'll look for in the April earnings results.

Valuation Metrics

We've come to the discussion on valuation and price forecasts now, and based on my worksheet below, I gave this stock a hold rating in this section. The inputs in this worksheet are all from Seeking Alpha data as of Monday.

BDN appears very undervalued compared to the peer average in terms of forward P/FFO and mostly in line when it comes to P/B, so the market seems to be considerably undervaluing its portfolio, lacking confidence in offices perhaps. This is why I would not call this an undervaluation opportunity to buy into now but a real picture of legitimate market concerns about office space.

As I said earlier, this REIT has rather muted margins vs. peers, considerable balance sheet risk, and a tenant retention story not yet convincing. Also, my price forecast only expects a +3% upside on its share price, not quite enough to be bullish yet. Hence, I am in the neutral territory here.

Technical Charting

In this final section, I move away from fundamental analysis for a moment and talk about a technical analysis topic, looking for buy/sell/hold patterns in the chart below, and my view is that the pattern indicates a bearish trend.

The key points I would point out would be that this stock appears to be on an extended bearish streak and not simply a short-term dip to take advantage of. For instance, both moving averages are continuing to trend downward, and the share price has been trending near or below the 200-day SMA (long-term moving average). From a purely technical pattern, if ignoring all the fundamentals discussed earlier, it would clearly be a sell.

While this section certainly hurts the overall score, some stronger fundamentals mentioned earlier help move the needle for the overall rating into the hold range for now.

Risks And Challenge To Thesis

Considering that my overall rating is a hold, I think a challenge to such a neutral rating could come from the fact that this stock is trading well under book value and that could set it up for a takeover by a much bigger REIT or perhaps private equity and getting taken private, with the goal of a turnaround.

While there is no news of such plans by anyone as of the writing of this article, I can see how some may challenge my overly cautious view, given that this REIT has developed lots of premium, modern office properties in places like Austin, PA, and northern Virginia, and as a company is trading below book value, at a discount essentially. In such a case, a more confident/bullish investor may consider the undervalued/discounted portfolio as highly attractive to a takeover bid, potentially driving upside to the share price if that happens.

Conclusion

To wrap up today, my rating was a bit less bearish than the latest consensus from both Wall Street and the quant rating. In my article, I showed what factors pull up this stock into the hold/neutral range for now, and I think the most positive impact comes from new lease growth in Q4 and FY25, showing there is some momentum in the office niche after all.

I would keep this on a watchlist of office REITs to consider if some of those metrics show improvement, and the long-term bet to "hold" this stock further is essentially betting that the bright spots emerging in the office niche continue to blossom. Until then, I remain on the fence and am not buying.

data.title: I Win Big For Every Dividend I Collect
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

In ancient Greece, major cities had an "agora," which was a public space where people were free to congregate. It served as a marketplace for people to trade goods and services and as a gathering point for public announcements, the military, religious ceremonies, and more. In many ways, an "agora" is no different from what might be called a "town center," "square," or "market." Every society has its public spaces where people get together for various purposes.

What made the agora of ancient Greece distinct was the openness and popularity of philosophical and political debate. It was in those agoras that Socrates and Plato made their marks on the world.

One of the things I love about the internet is that it allows for the spirited and open debate of ideas. The internet is our modern version of the agora, where we can seek out to challenge our ideas and honestly debate the merits of an issue.

The issue I would like to submit for discussion today is "total return." It's a concept that many people love to talk about in the comment threads. It is a concept that I believe is routinely misapplied.

Let's discuss.

Total Return Vs. Dividend Investing

Many love to argue that there is some difference between "total return investing" and "dividend investing." The reality is that there isn't. A dividend is total return. In fact, a dividend is realized total return. The total return of an investment is:

Total Return = (Price you sell + dividends you collected) / price you paid

If you sell at $15, collected $0 in dividends, and paid $10, your total return is 50%.

($15 + $0) / $10 = 1.5, a 50% gain.

If you sell for $10, collected $5 in dividends, and paid $10, your total return is 50%.

($10 + $5) / $10 = 1.5, a 50% gain.

If you sell for $8, collected $7 in dividends, and paid $10, your total return is 50%.

($8 + $7) / $10 = 1.5, a 50% gain.

In all three scenarios, you invested $10 and received $15 in cash flow. Whether the cash flow came from selling shares or from dividends is irrelevant to the total return calculation.

Flying High

What many really mean to say is that investors prefer investments that don't pay a meaningful dividend over investments that do pay a meaningful dividend. There is a huge difference between investments that pay little to no dividend and investments that pay a high dividend relative to their share price.

First and foremost, an investment that is paying a high dividend can't trade at a high valuation. This is by definition because dividend yield is calculated using share price. Dividend yield is the dividend an investment pays divided by the current share price. As the price goes up, the dividend yield comes down.

Consider Nvidia (NVDA), which pays a dividend, but it isn't a "dividend stock" because its yield is a paltry 0.02%. To yield 5%, NVDA would have to pay a dividend of $8.65/share. Well, NVDA is expected to earn only $8.29/share.

Investors aren't buying NVDA for the dividend; they are buying for the growth and, as a result, are willing to pay much higher prices than any dividend investor would pay. The valuation upside for a growth stock is constrained only by the imaginations of investors. The valuation upside for a dividend stock is constrained by the reality that investors looking for income can take their money and invest in US Treasuries or other income alternatives.

Investors are drawn to these stocks because the price upside can be immense. And it is a valid investment strategy that can lead to very large gains. It is a strategy that, in recent years, has paid off very well.

The S&P 500 was trading at a P/E ratio of 21.6 at the end of February: Source

This is much higher than it has been for most of the past 40+ years. The only time it has been meaningfully higher was the late 1990s, which we now call the "Dot-com Bubble." In fact, the current valuation is almost exactly where it was in 2001, shortly before the bubble burst.

When you are sitting at a high valuation, looking in the rearview mirror, investments in the S&P 500 will be looking great. They look great because they are really highly priced. If you are selling today, then you are likely realizing a very good total return. You will be hard-pressed to find dividend stocks that have seen similar upside, since they are trading at lower valuations.

So are you selling today?

Lessons From The Past

Consider one of our holdings, Royce Small-Cap Trust (RVT), a fund that invests in small-cap stocks with a Value style approach. RVT produces a hefty dividend for investors while investing in stocks that aren't in the S&P 500. While the S&P 500 is trading at high valuations, small-cap stocks aren't.

If you are sitting today looking backwards, many would say that RVT is inferior to the S&P 500 on a total return basis. It has underperformed over the past decade:

This is very similar to what happened in the decade from 1991 through 2000.

In both cases, RVT did ok. It has a reasonably good return, but the S&P 500 outperformed. An investor looking at charts in 2000 would have noted material underperformance for RVT on the charts. Many would have avoided it for that reason, and many did.

Yet, in the 10 years following it, RVT made up the lost ground and then some, as the S&P 500 had a "lost decade":

So, if you are a "total return" investor, then clearly the high-yielding RVT was a vastly superior investment to the low-yielding S&P 500.

History doesn't repeat, but it certainly can rhyme. Investors who are sitting there looking at the 5- and 10-year charts and deciding to buy the stocks that have outperformed during that period need to consider how much of that return has been driven by an increase in valuation as opposed to an increase in earnings.

In 2000, we now know it was a huge error for investors to assume that high valuations would continue indefinitely.

Realizing Returns

Let's discuss the concept of realized versus unrealized returns. Many people I talk to love to tell me how much their unrealized returns are. In the words of Shania Twain:

"That don't impress me much."

As we saw above, unrealized returns can remain unrealized for a long time. Realizing returns has a fundamental impact on your investment results that isn't observable on most charting software.

Consider this comparison of RVT and Vanguard 500 Index Fund (VFINX) from January 1991 through February 2026:

A $100k original investment, and through the swings, they end up in roughly the same place. Sometimes RVT outperformed, and sometimes VFINX outperformed. They happen to end within spitting distance of each other.

RVT comes out slightly ahead at 11.16% CAGR versus 11.06% CAGR. But some might point out that RVT has a higher standard deviation and larger drawdowns:

A reasonable investor might argue that the higher volatility offsets the ultimately small higher total return. And they're right, volatility is something that has to be considered because it isn't useful to have a high total return if it is gone when you go to realize it.

Let's look at what happens if an investor puts in $100k in 1991 and then withdraws $7,000 in year one, increasing that amount each year with inflation. After all, what is the point of investing if you are never taking your gains out?

Remember how RVT was essentially the same as VFINX, with just 0.1% CAGR separating them? As soon as we start assuming withdrawals, that changes. They go from being roughly the same to RVT being materially better as soon as those gains start to be realized.

The difference in performance is larger in both gross dollars and as a percentage:

VFINX went from underperforming by about $100k with a $4 million portfolio value to underperforming by $500k with a $1 million portfolio value. It's also worth mentioning that the size of the withdrawals will change the significance of the impact, but even with tiny withdrawals, the same effect happens.

The reason is simple: RVT pays a dividend that was high enough to fund the withdrawals most of the time. As a result, the excess dividends were being used to buy more shares, while relatively few shares were sold to fund withdrawals. The VFINX investment never covered the withdrawal with dividends. It was selling shares every single year.

Conclusion: Invest for the Long Term

35 years sounds like a long time, and it is. From 1991 to today, the world has changed immensely. Yet if you retire at 65, withdrawing for 35 years puts you at 100 years old. Not everyone is going to make it to the Centenarian Club, but the number of people who are making it increased 50% in the U.S. from 2010 to 2020. As medical technology improves, that number will likely keep rising.

The reality is that you have to plan for the possibility that you survive. The last thing you want to be doing at 100 is going and looking for a new career! That means investors need to plan on withdrawing from their investments indefinitely.

When looking at "total return," too many investors stare at unrealized total returns. Charts that assume the investor never withdraws a penny and all the dividends are reinvested. They don't ask the question:

"What happens if I start to realize that return?"

When you start assuming withdrawals, the math changes dramatically. Investments in high flyers relying on growing valuations are capable of producing much higher paper returns. Yet, they rely on investors keeping their capital invested and not withdrawing anything during the inevitable downswings. If you are an investor looking to take cash out, that's a problem.

Our focus is on realizing returns – taking cash out of our portfolio on a regular and recurring basis. We do this because that's what is going to happen in the real world of retirement. Don't tell me about unrealized total return; tell me how much return you are realizing and using to pay your bills.

data.title: Alcoa Has A Lot Going For It That Justifies Further Upside
data.description:
One company that has had exceptional performance in the time that I have been bullish about it is aluminum producer Alcoa Corporation (AA). Back in July of 2024, I wrote a bullish article about the company. My opinion at the time was that the efforts that management was employing aimed at boosting profitability, on top of certain acquisitions and underlying fundamental performance, justified market-beating upside. Since reaffirming it in that article as a 'buy' candidate, the stock has appreciated by 72.7%. That dwarfs the 16.9% increase that the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. But that's nothing. Since I originally turned bullish on it in February of that same year, the stock has risen 162.8%. The market, meanwhile, is up only 28.7%.

This year alone, shares of the business are up 24.8%. While in prior years the company was carried by strong fundamental performance, a lot of the recent upside can be attributed to geopolitical issues. So what we have here are two stories baked into one. You have a company that has demonstrated, over the last few years, fantastic operational performance and revenue growth. Plus, now you have a geopolitical catalyst that could prove even more bullish for shareholders. As great as all of this is, however, I will say that upside potential likely is more limited than it was in the past. I won't go so far as to downgrade the stock considering the opportunities that abound. I do think that it can outperform the market for the foreseeable future, but the degree of outperformance probably won't be as extreme as what we have seen in the past.

One last thing that I would like to touch on, near the end of this article, is the fact that management will soon be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, analysts actually seem a bit pessimistic, with revenue and profitability likely to decline. While that is certainly possible, I would be a bit surprised considering how the company has performed as of late. But hopefully we will get some more insight into fundamental health and management's thoughts on the industry more broadly.

A Great Business With A Great Track Record

I would like to break up this article into three separate sections. The first one that you are reading right now will be dedicated instead to past financial performance in order to demonstrate the kind of quality that I am talking about when I say that Alcoa Corporation is a great business. The second is focused more on the catalysts that exist for the business right now. And then finally, I would like to touch briefly on earnings expectations for the upcoming release on April 16th. I think perhaps the best place to start here, however, would be to talk briefly about what the company is and what it does.

For those not familiar, it is really comprised of two separate segments. One of these is its Alumina segment, which involves the company's bauxite mines and alumina refineries. These operations include the mining of bauxite and other aluminous ores, not to mention the refining, production, and sale of smelter-grade and non-metallurgical alumina. And then we have its Aluminum segment, which focuses instead on its smelting and casting operations, plus certain energy production activities that the business engages in. Aluminum is one of the most important metals on the planet, used heavily across countless industries. This includes the transportation sector, the energy space, construction, packaging, consumer durables, and so much more. As a major player in the industry, the company has established operations all across the world. Examples include Australia, Brazil, Spain, Canada, Iceland, Norway, the US, and elsewhere.

Over the last three years now, revenue for the business has expanded nicely from $10.55 billion to $12.83 billion. This growth has been pretty uniform from one year to the next. From 2023 to 2024, sales increased 12.7%. And from 2024 to 2025, they grew 7.9%. This can really be attributed to higher prices. For instance, the Alumina segment reported a rise in revenue from $5.26 billion to $6.56 billion. This came as third-party alumina shipments grew revenue from $3.13 billion to $3.71 billion. During this window of time, the price realized per metric ton of alumina grew from $358 to $415. But that's not all. The aluminum segment reported a rise in sales from $6.94 billion to $8.38 billion. This came as average pricing jumped from $2,828 per metric ton to $3,376 per metric ton.

In general, pricing for aluminum, which is publicly known and easily accessible, has been on the rise in recent years. The chart above illustrates as much. Although I will get to more recent issues in a moment, it is worth noting that, so far this year, prices are up 16.6%. The great thing about price increases is that they have a tendency, absent a corresponding rise in costs, to boost profitability. That's why the business went from a net loss of $651 million in 2023 to a net profit of $1.16 billion in 2025. Other profitability metrics improved also. Operating cash flows skyrocketed from $91 million to $1.19 billion. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $385 million to $1.66 billion. Finally, EBITDA for the company grew from $536 million to $1.98 billion. This year, with higher prices, there is the opportunity for further improvement on the bottom line. But it's not just pricing that is likely to be of benefit to shareholders. Overall alumina production this year is expected to come in at between 0.6% and 2.7% above what the company saw last year. Aluminum production, meanwhile, should be between 3.5% and 12.1% above what the company saw last year.

Even if we ignore the prospect of higher prices and higher activity leading to higher profitability, the stock is priced at levels that I would consider to be reasonable. In the chart above, you can see how the company is priced using results from the last two completed fiscal years. These are respected multiples that typically would fit into the undervalued category as I describe it. But of course, in such a commoditized market with extreme price volatility, I do tend to be a bit more cautious. I have learned plenty of lessons over the years about not taking that approach when it comes to oil, iron ore, and other commodities.

There Are Real Catalysts Here

The way I see it, there are a couple of real catalysts that could supercharge performance for investors moving forward. The most reliable is the expectation that demand for aluminum should continue to expand from this point on. In the chart above, you can see, for the last several years now, total global aluminum production. This is primary output and does not factor in secondary sources. If you do the math, then from 1973 through 2025, total global primary aluminum production has increased 3.6% per annum. In all likelihood, we should see this trend continue.

Unfortunately, the only good data that is available that I can find is a bit old. It comes from 2022. But according to that source, it was estimated that, between 2020 and 2030, total global demand for aluminum would jump by 38.6%. That's an annualized increase of about 3.3%. A lot of this should come from the transportation sector, thanks in large part to investments in electric vehicles. Overall demand is expected to rise by 59.3%. But there are other spaces where demand should grow also. Investments in alternative energy, for instance, should help to boost demand for the use of aluminum in the production of electricity by 50%. The construction sector should see growth of 21.6%. Packaging should jump by 45.8%. We should see double-digit growth when it comes to other categories as well, such as consumer durables and machinery and equipment.

All of this data has been known for years. However, it is likely that some of it will require revisions thanks to the boom in data centers that the world is experiencing right now. Outside of the demand picture, however, there is another catalyst. And that is what is currently driving the narrative. In late February of this year, the US government launched a war against Iran. And even though the main thing that has been in the headlines as a result of this conflict has been oil, since about 18.8% of global oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz, other sectors are also being impacted. The fertilizer industry is one such example. However, aluminum is another.

According to one source, between 8% and 9% of all global aluminum production flows through the Strait of Hormuz. That data is consistent with what I have seen when looking at publicly available figures. For context, there is a group called the Gulf Cooperation Council. It is a collection of 6 Arab monarchies in the Middle East. Specifically, I am talking about Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, the UAE, Qatar, Bahrain, and Oman. It was established back in 1981 and is dedicated to furthering the economic goals of the nations that are part of it.

As the chart above illustrates, back in 2010, this collection of countries was responsible for about 6.4% of global primary aluminum production. That number has increased over time. It did end up peaking at 8.9% in 2019 and 2020. And since then, it has dropped to about 8.3%. But that is still substantial, and it translates to an annualized increase in overall global supply of 5.6% for the 15 years ending in 2025. In the commodities market, price swings can become significant even if changes in supply, demand, or both are relatively modest. Anybody who follows the oil sector as closely as I do, for instance, can vouch for that.

On March 19th of this year, because of the conflict going on against Iran, news broke that aluminum producers are looking to reroute exports to safer ports. Smelters decided to cut production because of the conflict. For instance, Aluminum Bahrain mentioned that it was considering trucking its product through Saudi Arabia to ports on the Red Sea or to Oman. And both it and Qatar's Qatalum have made substantial reductions to their output due to the difficulty in arranging safe transport.

This came just days after the leading industry association for alumina and bauxite in Australia, which is responsible for about 25% of global bauxite production and which serves as a major supplier to smelters in the Middle East, said that a bottleneck was becoming increasingly likely because of the conflict. It doesn't help that Iran has been active in attacking steel plants as it lashes out at its neighbors during this time. In fact, on March 28th, there were attacks on two separate steel plants. One of these, the Al-Taweelah smelting facility in the UAE, is responsible for about 1.6 million metric tons of cast metal production annually, while Aluminum Bahrain produces a similar amount.

There are rumors circulating that the war against Iran might be coming to an end soon. But the devil is in the details here. We do not know what the outcome will be, and there are legitimate concerns that it might result in Iran controlling the Strait of Hormuz in a way that it never has in recent memory. Until the dust settles, this conflict should prove to be a net positive for companies such as Alcoa Corporation, which stand to benefit from the fact that none of their facilities have been compromised and from the fact that they are able to hike prices as a result.

Keep An Eye On Earnings

The last thing that I would like to touch on before ending this article would be expectations that analysts have regarding upcoming earnings figures. Right now, the belief is that the company will report revenue of $3.30 billion. That would actually be down from the $3.37 billion that management reported a year earlier. This would likely lead to a decline in profitability, with the current consensus being that earnings per share will decline from $2.07 to $1.53. That would mean a drop in net income from $548 million to $404 million. In the table below, you can see certain cash flow metrics for the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. If management does report lower revenue and profits, then these will likely worsen as well. However, I would urge investors to pay careful attention to what management says regarding the state of the industry when data does come out. Because I would make the case that expectations there will be even more important than short-term financial results.

Takeaway

Based on the data that's available right now, I would say that there are plenty of reasons to be optimistic about Alcoa Corporation. Some of these are fundamental. Some are long-term structural positives for the company. And some of them are geopolitical concerns. I am worried about the global economy more broadly, most notably the US, which is likely to enter into a recession, and China, which is slowing down. But unless something truly catastrophic happens on that front, I would argue that the picture is sufficiently positive to maintain a soft 'buy' rating on the stock.

data.title: Market Fear Is Rising: 2 Cheap Dividend Stocks To Reward You With Income
data.description:
Introduction

So far, the first three months or so in 2026 have seen market volatility increase due to a number of factors.

Particularly, the war with Iran has resulted in economic uncertainty. And going forward, I think this will continue unless we come to an agreement.

If we don't, then I believe it's likely we'll continue to see market volatility, recession odds increase, or the potential to see stagflation. However, this makes income investing even more attractive.

Especially in stocks backed by strong fundamentals with higher yields and the likelihood to continue rewarding shareholders with reliable income.

In this article, I discuss two attractively priced stocks that are likely to do just that for the foreseeable future.

Knowing When To Buy

As someone who's been investing for some time now, buying quality stocks isn't all that difficult. To me, the most difficult part about investing is knowing when to buy those quality stocks.

Buying stocks when everyone else is interested is a way to either underperform or see muted portfolio returns. Basically, it's best to buy when others are fearful, like now.

As previously mentioned, the war with Iran has investors spooked. According to the CNN Fear & Greed Index, the market is experiencing extreme fear at the moment.

As a result, the overall market has experienced increased volatility through the first three months plus. This is after closing up over 16% in 2025. So far, all the momentum has been lost.

And until we see more clarity around the war, I believe this will continue. But in my opinion, now is the best time to be a dividend investor. Especially if you can buy stocks with yields above the rate of inflation with the likelihood to continue growing them.

The two in this article are attractively priced with well-covered dividends. And both have solid dividend yields. Without further ado, let's get into them.

Omnicom Group (OMC) - Fwd Yield 4.33%

At the current price, Omnicom Group trades at a forward P/E of 6.82x. While the stock has underperformed over a 1-, 5-, and 10-year period, I believe this is subject to change going forward.

The reason is their recent acquisition of Interpublic Group. With the acquisition, OMC is now the world's leading marketing & sales company.

As a result, Omnicom doubled their annual run rate synergies from $750 million to $1.5 billion over the next 2 1/2 years. For 2026, they're expecting $900 million. You can see growth over the next 5 years is strong, with a CAGR of 14%, with growth expected to accelerate through 2028.

And if they can manage to achieve such growth, then I expect significant upside in the next 2 to 3 years. According to FastGraphs, Omnicom Group looks severely undervalued here.

If they can return to their normal P/E of 14.48x, fair for a company of their caliber, then investors are looking at more than 100% upside from the current price of $74 a share.

Furthermore, the company also announced an accelerated buyback program worth $2.5 billion. In 2025, management bought back $708 million worth of shares. And at the current share price, I expect management to take advantage.

So far, the IPG acquisition seems to be paying off. OMC saw organic growth of 9.3%, up from 5.2% the year prior. This was led by higher growth in their Media & Advertising segment, which grew from 7.9% to 14.9%.

They still saw negative growth in two of their segments, led by Branding & Retail Commerce, which was down 15.8%. Their Public Relations segment was down 2.2%.

However, free cash flow was strong and actually grew double digits to $2.23 billion. Dividends paid amounted to $550 million, giving them a conservative payout ratio of 33%.

This gives the company plenty of room for future dividend increases. Another indicator of management's optimism is their 14.3% dividend increase to $0.80 a share back in November.

The IPG acquisition did increase the company's leverage ratio from 0.70x to 3.1x. But due to their retained cash flow and robust liquidity of $6.9 billion, I suspect management to focus on deleveraging in the coming years. Their liquidity also gives them the ability to satisfy their 2026 debt maturity of $1.4 billion due next month.

Verizon (VZ) - Fwd Yield 5.63%

Although Verizon has performed well, I think the stock is attractively priced at a forward P/E of 10.22x. At the time of writing, VZ is up over 16% in the past year.

Like Omnicom, VZ reported a strong Q4 to close out 2025. With a new CEO, the telecom giant seems to be firing on all cylinders. Personally, I believe passive management is something that plagued VZ for years.

But now the company seems on the right track with $5 billion in cost-savings initiatives expected for 2026. Recently, VZ announced potentially cutting their NFL sponsorship.

Currently, VZ spends $250 million on sponsorships, so cutting this would set the telecom giant up for long-term success. For 2025, Verizon made slight improvements. One was by deleveraging the balance sheet to 2.2x.

They also announced a $25 billion buyback program, something I've been saying they needed for some time now. This, the higher dividend increase, and cost-cutting initiatives are reasons for the stock's share price jump in the past two months.

In 2025, Verizon's dividend was supported by strong cash flows. FCF was up slightly year-over-year from $19.8 billion to $20.1 billion. This was a result of higher cash from operations and lower capital expenditures.

VZ paid out $11.5 billion in dividends, giving them a conservative free cash flow payout of 57%. This gives them plenty of retained capital for reinvestments and share repurchases.

And going forward, this should lead to higher growth, which is what management is expecting going forward. EPS is expected in a range of $4.90 - $4.95. This represents a growth rate of 4.6% from 2025's $4.71.

This is significantly higher than their usual growth of 1%–2%. You can see from the chart below that analysts are anticipating higher growth through the next 5 years with a CAGR of 7.1%.

If Verizon can manage to achieve this, I foresee solid upside for the stock over the next 2 to 3 years. Currently, they offer roughly 50% from the current price of $50.49 a share.

What Could Go Wrong

Currently, both Omnicom Group and Verizon are coming into 2026 with solid momentum. Both have strong fundamentals, and their dividends are backed by growing cash flows. However, the ongoing war with Iran remains a mystery.

If this continues, this increases the chance of a recession. Or stagflation. And I suspect we would see more volatility for at least the near to medium term.

Treasury yields could spike, leading to potential underperformance in both companies. Although I see a higher chance of the FED not cutting rates as expected previously, the war also increases chances of a possible rate hike.

Going forward, this is something investors should continue to monitor. If we do see a rate hike, I foresee both Omnicom Group and Verizon possibly hitting new 52-week lows.

Conclusion

Although more volatility is likely in the near term, I think now is a good time to be an income-focused investor. Due to the potential to see a recession or stagflation, stocks with higher yields backed by conservative payout ratios and manageable debt could serve investors well.

Furthermore, OMC & VZ have made recent acquisitions, which positions them well for long-term growth. And I suspect their upside will follow once we manage to see more economic clarity, likely over the next 12 months.

data.title: AI Disruption Is Reshaping My Entire Investment Strategy
data.description:
I will go straight into it.

This is the chart that's reshaping my entire investment strategy:

It shows that, still today, about 84% of people have yet to even use AI.

I was just home for the weekend and met with my parents and their friends.

Most of them belong in that category.

Despite hearing every day about AI, they still haven't even tried using it. The reason is that people are stuck in their routines, and old habits take time to change.

This proves, in my opinion, that the AI revolution remains underhyped, not overhyped.

It has already started to disrupt lots of businesses, and that's with just 16% of people having begun to use it, and most of them are not using it efficiently. Less than 1% of people pay for an AI subscription, and I am willing to bet that the majority of those people are not making the most of it either.

What this means is that we are still incredibly early, with 80%+ not using AI at all and 99%+ of people not using it to its full capacity.

Despite that, it is already disrupting markets. SaaS companies were the first domino to fall, as these are 100% digital businesses, and AI can easily replicate them with very low resources. It is breaking barriers to entry, and competition is now exploding. Consider that over the past year alone, the number of apps released on the iOS store increased by 50%.

That's in just one year!

The market is a forward-looking machine, and it knows that if the cost and difficulty of developing new apps and SaaS-like businesses drop significantly, the competition will surge, pricing power will suffer, and this will at minimum lead to lower long-term retention and growth rates for established SaaS companies like Salesforce, Inc. (CRM), monday.com Ltd. (MNDY), Roper Technologies, Inc. (ROP), and Constellation Software, Inc. (CNSWF). This explains why they have all been crashing down:

But I want to emphasize again that this is based on 80%+ of people not using AI yet and 99%+ of people not using AI efficiently.

It is also based on AI models that are still rapidly improving each year. Remember that ChatGPT came out just 4 years ago, so these AIs could be far more powerful in just another year or two from now.

And I fear that as these models get more powerful and far more people start to actually properly use AI, it will have a massive impact on the stock market, and this prediction is reshaping my portfolio.

SaaS companies were the first to crash, but surely not the last.

I think that most stocks will suffer from AI disruption over the long run, and I am reconfiguring my portfolio ahead of that to hopefully avoid this AI-driven value destruction.

Professional service providers, healthcare, education, retail, entertainment, transportation, logistics, platforms, finance, consumer brands, etc.

All of it will be impacted, as I discussed in a recent article.

Here are just a few examples for you to think about:

  • Will people still buy as many cars when self-driving taxis are all over the place and available at a cost that's inferior to owning a personal car?
  • Will traditional retail stores still do as well in the future as AI makes the online marketing far more personalized and powerful, even as it also decreases the cost of shipping thanks to autonomous delivery vehicles?
  • Will the e-commerce companies themselves earn higher profits if they have to deal with far greater competition in the future, as anyone can now start an online shop with very few resources over a weekend?
  • Will service providers like law firms still manage to charge as much per hour if AI can answer simple questions for free, resulting in lower demand, and also make more complex tasks far easier, allowing individual lawyers to become solopreneurs, resulting in far more supply?
  • Will people still value consumer brands as much and be willing to pay steep premiums relative to white-label alternatives if AI makes it far easier to compare pricing and product characteristics, allowing consumers to make better purchase decisions, regardless of the logo?
  • Will movie studios, theaters, video game developers, etc., manage to earn as much profit in a world in which anyone can create a new hyper-personalized game or movie at a very low cost?
  • Will private schools still be able to charge huge tuition when a personal AI tutor can teach more efficiently at a very low cost, perfectly customized to your level of knowledge and pace of learning, which traditional education cannot do in a class setting?
  • Will people still pay tens of thousands each year for Bloomberg subscriptions when AI can build you similar platforms at a much lower cost?
  • How will logistics companies survive as new players with large fleets of self-driving vehicles cut prices?

I could go on and on, but you get the point.

Most businesses will suffer from AI in one way or another.

It is highly deflationary in nature. It breaks barriers to entry. Furthermore, it brings new, powerful competition. And it ultimately commoditizes businesses across the board, often giving the advantage to new AI-native businesses versus large and bloated companies with lots of slow, imperfect, and expensive human labor.

As AI gets better and more people start to use it over the years, the impact on the valuations of these businesses could be significant already in the near term.

The market has not recognized this yet, but as it will, stocks could crash just like those of SaaS companies, as the market is a forward-looking machine and will adjust long-term expectations.

So if that's my prediction, what am I investing in?

The answer is primarily in infrastructure-like real assets that AI cannot disrupt or replicate.

Think here of things that society will always need but are and will remain limited in supply, giving them sustainable and even growing pricing power that's inflation-protected.

Good examples of that include:

Timberland: We will always need wood for lots of different things, ranging from building materials to furniture, paper, packaging, etc. It is environmentally friendly, self-renewable, and cost-efficient. Moreover, the land itself also holds a lot of value. We are not making any more of it, and each year, some parts of timberland are converted into better-use projects, including housing and renewable energy, as AI requires a lot of it. Yet, publicly listed timberland REITs like Rayonier Inc. (RYN) are today trading at a huge 40% discount to their net asset value, offering a margin of safety and significant upside potential as more investors become more concerned about wealth preservation in a world getting disrupted by AI. While you wait for the upside, you also earn a 5.2% dividend yield.

E-commerce warehouses: We will always need buildings to store products and protect them from the elements and thieves. Moreover, e-commerce businesses require far more storage space than traditional retailers, and as AI makes online marketing far more potent, I expect e-commerce companies to steal market share, resulting in even more demand for warehouses. At the same time, it also breaks barriers to entry, and I expect this to result in an explosion in small new e-commerce business formation, all of which will need storage space. Despite that, some leading landlords in this space are trading at near-decade low valuations with 30%+ discounts to their net asset values. Rexford Industrial Realty, Inc. (REXR) is a good example of that. It has great long-term growth prospects, and yet, it now offers a 5.2% dividend yield and significant upside potential as its valuation eventually recovers closer to its historic norm.

One last example:

Renewable energy infrastructure: The need for energy is surging as a result of the AI revolution, as data centers consume a lot of electricity. Yet, AI cannot just magically produce more energy. It requires infrastructure, which takes years to build and requires a lot of capital, land, planning, and permits, which limit the supply. As a result, listed renewable energy infrastructure companies like Brookfield Renewable Partners L.P. (BEP) and Brookfield Renewable Corporation (BEPC) are expected to grow their FCF per share by about 10% annually going forward, and despite that, they are still offering a 5% dividend yield. Even ignoring any upside potential, the long-term total returns from the yield and growth could be very compelling.

I think that this type of essential infrastructure company that pays a high yield, enjoys steady growth, and cannot be disrupted by AI will surge in value as more investors come to the same realization that AI will disrupt most businesses.

Now, you still have the chance to invest in these companies before they become the hot new thing to own in a world of AI disruption.

We are positioning our portfolio accordingly at High Yield Landlord to not only protect ourselves but also to capitalize on this upside potential.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: MarketWise: Cheap Valuation, But Structural Risks Remain
data.description:
Introduction

In my view, MarketWise right now is in a transitional stage in which improving cash flows are masking the structural subscriber base weakening.

MarketWise, Inc. (MKTW) is currently demonstrating a clear difference between the worsening GAAP financial results and the improving operational cash flows, which shows an active business model reorganization. Based on the fiscal 2025 report, the company's net income shrank 19.7% to $328.1 million, while adjusted net income fell 31.2% to $64.0 million, though this drop is a consequence of recognizing past sales and doesn't reflect the current business health.

However, billings, which better represent the real demand, grew 13.4% to $271.2 million, and in Q4 even recorded a jump of 42% compared to last year. Even though the number of paid subscriptions throughout the year shrunk 26.1% to 374 thousand, average revenue per user grew 70% to around $650, which shows a successful pivot to a higher value client segment and effective monetization.

Operating cash flows in 2025 reached $45 million, radically improving from the negative $22 million in 2024. The company ended the year with $70 million in cash and no debt. The current forward P/E ratio is reaching around 6.2, suggesting that the market still values skeptically subscriber base stability, despite the fact that the net revenue retention rate recovered to 91%.

In 2026, management is forecasting a $300 million billings level and is planning to pay dividends of $1.80 per share, which makes up an annual yield of 13%. Rejecting a takeover bid of $17.25 per share, management is demonstrating confidence that current capitalization underestimates the operational leverage and the ability to generate free cash flow on a stabilized subscriber base.

Business Overview

MarketWise operates as a content platform for individual investors, managing 12 main brands and offering over 160 different investment products. The main source of revenue is subscription fees, which at the end of 2025 made up the biggest part of the $420 million yearly revenues. The company's business model is based on aggressive client attraction through direct marketing, for which they spend over 45% of their total revenues.

As of the newest data, the company's total subscriber count stabilized at the 374 thousand level, which is low compared to the 2021 peak when the company had almost 1 million. This drop suggests that the traditional newsletter model faces structural challenges because of the free, content-rich market.

The product rotation is a critical element when trying to maintain the APRU indicator, which is reaching around $650. The company is constantly moving users from cheap entry-level subscriptions to high-value products, which are worth from $2,000 to $10,000. Such rotation allows to compensate for the decreasing new user traffic, though it increases dependency on a small loyal client group, which generates the majority of billings.

At the start of 2026, financial indicators are showing that operational margin remains pressured due to increasing costs of customer retention and the decreasing effectiveness of attracting new subscribers. A P/S ratio reaching around 0.8 reflects the market's skepticism about the long-term growth sustainability and the business model's sensitivity to market cycles. If product rotation does not manage to generate enough cash flows to finance operations, the company's valuation could shrink even further, because investors are prioritizing profitability over stagnant user numbers.

In my view, such a business model still currently works, though it is becoming more and more dependent on a narrow, high-value customer segment, which, in the long term, raises some serious questions about the growth sustainability.

Valuation

MarketWise's valuation in 2026 reflects market uncertainty in the company's ability to stabilize revenue flows. The P/S ratio is around 0.8x, which is well below the financial information sector's average, which is around 2.5. For comparison, The New York Times (NYT) is at a 3.8 PS ratio, and this is justified by its ability to maintain a low client loss level and consistent subscriber growth. In MKTW's case, the low P/S ratio is not a buying signal but rather a structural problem indicator, because the company's revenues are directly dependent on the success of aggressive marketing campaigns and not organic loyalty.

An EV/EBITDA ratio in the low single-digit range seems attractive compared to MSCI Inc. or S&P Global, which are over 20x, though MKTW doesn't have the same level of intellectual property protection or competitive advantage. The company's P/E ratio remains unstable due to one-off restructuring costs and fluctuating client acquisition costs, which in 2025 made up 45% of revenues.

Compared to asset management companies such as T. Rowe Price, MKTW's business model has a higher leverage risk: when sales are decreasing, fixed costs quickly destroy net profit. The current valuation will remain as such until the company is able to prove that it can generate positive free cash flows without constantly burning capital to find new users.

Risks

MarketWise's risk in 2026 remains directly tied to the fundamental subscriber base decrease process and a complex revenue recognition structure. Even though in 2025 billings grew 13% to $271 million, GAAP net income dropped 20% to $328 million. This gap reflects the expiration of long-term subscriptions sold during the 2021-2022 peak, which, for the company, is hard to compensate for with new sales in the current environment.

The biggest concern is the decline in the number of paid subscribers; in a year it shrunk 26%. Even though management is trying to fight this drop by raising the prices, which is why average revenue per customer reached around $650, such a strategy raises the dependency on a small, wealthier customer base, which is very sensitive to macroeconomic shocks and market volatility.

Also, the reputational and regulatory risks remain due to past misconduct in subsidiaries, which limits institutional investment confidence. Aggressive capital return policy, including the $1.80 dividend per share and $50 million share buyback program, raises some questions about financial flexibility, as net income in 2025 dropped 31% to $64 million.

Thesis summary

In my view, MarketWise Inc. is currently in a transitional stage, where the market's skepticism is fundamentally justified, though it could also hide a short term opportunity. On one hand, it is clear that the business is successfully moving from a mass model to a higher value customer segment, which is shown by the strong growth of ARPU and the improving cash flows. This is not a trivial change because it shows that the company can get more value from the smaller client base, which theoretically allows it to stabilize profitability even when the user count is dropping.

On the other hand, this model has a clear limit. The dependency on a smaller but wealthier client circle raises the business's sensitivity to market cycles and sentiment. If investor activity drops, these types of products are the first to suffer. Also, aggressive marketing as the main growth engine shows that there is almost no organic growth, which in the long term raises doubts about the sustainability of the business.

Valuing it at the current price, the stock seems cheap by the traditional multiples, though this is not a classical value situation. The market discounts the structural problems and not the short-term weakness. In my view, this is more of a waiting situation until the company shows that it is able to generate stable free cash flow without constant customer acquisition; the upside is limited, and the risk remains high.

data.title: 2 Undervalued 8-9% Yields The Market Is Sleeping On After Energy's Huge Rally
data.description:
The war in Iran has caused energy prices to skyrocket, and with it, it has sent the broader energy (XLE) and midstream infrastructure (MLPX) sectors soaring.

As a result, a sector that has been my highest conviction investment since last year is now no longer filled with the bargains that it once was.

That being said, there are a few names that have been overlooked in the run-up in the sector that I think remain compelling buying opportunities today. In this article, I detail two high-yielding energy infrastructure opportunities (AMLP) that are still attractive buys today.

Quality Counterparty, Commodity Tailwinds

The first one I'm going to discuss is Hess Midstream LP (HESM). HESM is highly concentrated in terms of its counterparty, as it is largely beholden to Chevron (CVX) as well as the Bakken for its business. While the lack of counterparty and geographical diversification is generally negative, CVX is one of the very highest quality energy companies in the world, which makes its contracts very dependable in terms of being able to withstand a prolonged energy downturn. At the same time, oil production in the Bakken is one of the least profitable basins today, especially in the areas that HESM services. Therefore, the production outlook for that portion of its business is uncertain.

The good news is that natural gas volumes make up about 75% of HESM's revenues, so even if the oil side of its business declines over time, the natural gas business should be able to do quite well. This is especially true given that it is likely we are entering a natural gas production boom in the United States to satisfy energy needs for AI as well as export demand from trading partners, especially in the wake of the world war. In fact, leaders in Asia and Europe are likely to be much more disposed to buy energy from North America rather than from the Middle East, given that the Straits of Hormuz is no longer a reliable pathway for energy supplies to flow.

An 8.1% Yield Nobody Is Talking About

This backdrop makes HESM compelling right now, given that it also currently offers an 8.1% next 12-month dividend yield. That is very attractive at a time when many peers have soared higher, which in turn has pushed their yields lower, while HESM's dividend yield remains in line with or even slightly above its five-year average.

Not only that, but HESM issues a 1099 tax form rather than a K-1, which means that if you want to compare it to 1099-issuing peers, its dividend yield looks even more attractive, since virtually all of those, such as Antero Midstream (AM), Oneok (OKE), and Kinder Morgan (KMI), have dividend yields below 5% today.

HESM also has a lot going for it elsewhere. For example, its capex is expected to decline sharply this year, at about 40% lower than 2025's capex spending. Its capex should decline even further in 2027 and 2028, which would cause free cash flow generation to soar higher. This, in turn, should leave management plenty of cash to return to shareholders through continued strong mid-single-digit or even higher dividend per share growth, while also having substantial cash flow to continue buying back stock aggressively and paying down debt to continue driving leverage lower, below its already very conservative ~3.0x net debt to EBITDA target level.

The other thing to keep in mind is that its minimum volume commitment contracts with CVX do not expire until 2028, which gives it several years to de-leverage further, buy back a significant number of shares, and return a lot of capital to shareholders via its dividend, while also waiting for energy prices to settle to a level that CVX will be able to make a decision about where it will be heading with its oil business in the Bakken. Even in a negative scenario for HESM in which CVX materially curtails its oil production activities in the areas that HESM services, the number of HESM shares and the amount of debt that will have been repurchased by that point, along with dividends paid out, should help to provide a significant margin of safety to help offset potential headwinds suffered at that point.

Sure, HESM is not risk-free, but given its high dividend yield, its very low 7x EV to EBITDA multiple, its 15.9% free cash flow yield, and its expected inflation-beating dividend growth alongside aggressive buybacks for the foreseeable future, HESM looks like a very attractive buy right now. This is especially true given that its stock price has underperformed the broader sector, even though it is one of the more commodity-price-sensitive investment theses in the midstream infrastructure space.

Note that its cash flows are fully supported by minimum volume commitments and other contractual guarantees. However, the terms and the likelihood of renewal of those contracts are dependent on energy prices; hence, why I think a higher-for-longer energy price environment would likely be very favorable for HESM.

The 9%+ Yield The Market Is Sleeping On

The other opportunity I'm going to discuss is Western Midstream Partners LP (WES), which currently yields 9.1% on a next 12-month basis. In fact, its distribution yield remains well above its five-year average, despite the broader sector seeing substantial yield compression in recent weeks and months.

Meanwhile, WES's business remains strong fundamentally, with a 3.18x leverage ratio that is among the most conservative in the sector. It just came off posting all-time records for adjusted EBITDA and also posted strong free cash flow numbers that came in above the high end of its guidance. On top of that, its debt maturities are well laddered, with the vast majority of its debt not maturing until 2029 or later.

Additionally, management has signaled that its capex this year is lower, which means more free cash flow generation, which should free up the company to deleverage and/or allocate capital more opportunistically than it would otherwise. At the same time, they are forecasting a bit weaker growth due to a weaker commodity price environment. However, the recent spike in energy prices should boost growth if energy prices remain higher over time, and yet it seems like the market is not pricing this in at all, even though it is pricing in higher commodity prices for many of its peers.

Some Of The Rare Wide-Margin-Of-Safety Midstream Buys Left

Between its investment-grade credit rating, low leverage ratio, the expected continued distribution growth at a low to mid-single-digit annualized pace, and the potential for future growth across its natural gas, oil, and water businesses, I think that WES presents a compelling buying opportunity here. After all, with a yield above 9%, it does not need to generate much in the way of growth to deliver mid-teens annualized total returns, especially if you can get some valuation multiple expansion. I think the odds of that happening are quite high, thus making WES one of the rare wide-margin-of-safety investments in the midstream space remaining today.

Of course, neither of these investments is risk-free, and as I already mentioned, they are at least somewhat commodity-price sensitive when it comes to their growth, and HESM in particular is beholden to CVX's decision-making regarding its future in the Bakken. On top of that, Occidental Petroleum (OXY) still owns a considerable number of units in WES, so if it were to further reduce its exposure, this would likely weigh on WES units in the near term. However, OXY recently made a deal with WES that significantly reduced its unit count, so this helps reduce this risk, at least in the near term.

Overall, though, with their high and well-covered yields, strong balance sheets, and potential for material future growth, I think HESM and WES are laggards that are worth buying in the wake of the recent very strong energy and midstream infrastructure rallies. Thus, these are positions that we own in our seven to eight percent yielding total return, outperforming and below-market beta portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: FactSet Research Systems: Valuation Multiple Is Too Cheap
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on FactSet Research Systems (FDS) was a buy rating because I thought the stock was cheap relative to the growth profile (which was accelerating) and that it was turning AI into a monetization driver. After looking at Q2 2026, I think a buy rating is still warranted. The quarter showed that growth is accelerating across the recurring base, customer relationships are getting stickier through longer enterprise contracts, and newer solutions in AI, wealth, and dealmakers are gaining traction.

Earnings review

FDS reported Q2 2026 numbers yesterday, with revenue up ~7% y/y to $611 million. Organic revenue growth came in at 6.8%, an acceleration from 4.5% y/y in Q2 2025. Segment performance was solid. US annual sales volume [ASV] was up 7% y/y to $1.6 billion, while international ASV was up ~6% y/y to $844.3 million. Profit performance was a little weaker than the top line. Adj. EBIT saw $214.1 million, only slightly above $212.7 million in Q2 2025, while adj. EBIT margin fell to 35% from 37.3%. This was pretty much the same for adj. net income that came in at $165.3 million vs. $165 million last year, while adj. EPS was up slightly to $4.46.

The growth story got better

We now have a lot of tangible numbers to prove that the growth story is working out. Take the organic ASV growth, for instance. At 6.7%, that was the fourth consecutive quarter of acceleration, and that was driven by all major drivers (like retention, expansion, and new business adds across all geographies). Moreover, you see this strength across all regions. The sharp jump in sequential ASV growth also matters. FDS added $38 million sequentially this quarter from just $6.6 million in Q1 2026. That is a huge change, and I take that as a clean indicator of future revenue growth. If ASV growth is accelerating, revenue growth should follow in time.

Now, the growth quality is also better and more durable. First of all, direct seat-based exposure is now <20% of ASV because of minimums and bundling into enterprise agreements. For perspective, in Q2, the majority of renewed ASV came in the form of enterprise agreements or contracts with durations of >3 years, and the average renewal length extended by >30%. This should kill the narrative that FDS's long-term growth is heavily under pressure since this literally lengthens the runway and also shows that FDS is reducing reliance on traditional seat growth.

Secondly, it is increasingly evident that AI is a growth driver for FDS. We can see it showing up in customer behavior. Management said 48 of its top 50 clients are already using at least three of FDS’s AI solutions, and there are several more still in trials. Another interesting data point is that March API call volume was 3x February (i.e., 300% m/m growth). All of this points to clients adopting AI through the FDS platform, and they are not treating these tools as side experiments. This tells me that FDS’s AI tools are being plugged into workflows, and that would mean FDS has become much more embedded and more difficult to replace.

The moat looks deeper in wealth and dealmakers

I also think the moat is getting stronger in two areas: wealth and dealmakers.

In wealth, FDS managed to print organic ASV of 10% even against a tough y/y comparison because of the prior UBS win, driven by higher demand for the wealth platform as FDS becomes more integrated into clients’ daily workflows (through proposal generation and advisor dashboard solutions). Two good examples this quarter were one of FDS’s largest international wealth clients adopting the proposal generation solution on top of its existing portfolio monitoring module and a major Canadian wealth manager also adopting FDS’s real-time exchange data feed product. These examples matter because they show FDS is doing more inside the client workflow (more integrated) and not just selling another product to juice the growth figure. The same thing is happening in dealmakers. Organic ASV growth here was 8%, up from 6% in Q1, and the fact it is winning competitive displacements and renewing enterprise relationships tells me that FDS continues to have a superior product.

What makes this important is that both wealth and dealmakers are moving beyond simple product sales and positioning themselves deeper into clients' workflows. The way I see it, in wealth, modules like proposal generation, advisor dashboards, portfolio monitoring, and real-time data should be well received, as they really help with an advisor’s day-to-day process. For example, an advisor preparing for a client meeting can use proposal generation and portfolio monitoring together to review the client’s holdings, build a recommendation, and turn that into something ready to present. That is very different from just pulling background data. Once FDS is part of that workflow, it becomes harder to remove because the product is helping the client do the actual work, not just supplying background data. In dealmakers, we can see the same pattern as well. Sector content, aftermarket research, and banker productivity tools push FDS closer to how bankers source, analyze, and execute deals.

Valuation

FDS’s share remains extremely cheap, in my opinion. The market appears to completely disregard the fact that FDS is thriving in this environment, one where market participants' AI is going to completely displace FDS. This is evidently not the case. Revenue growth continues to accelerate to high single digits, and the number of user growth actually accelerated y/y to >10% in Q2 2026. This is not the profile of a business in perpetual decline (or under heavy competitive pressure). Management even upgraded their FY2026 guide to reflect this strength, which gives me more confidence that my 7/9% growth assumption and 28% earnings margin for FY27/28 are plausible. The biggest variant today is market perception, which is reflected through the multiples, and that has been weak. FDS now trades at just ~12x forward PE, and that doesn’t make sense given this is a business that can still compound adj. EPS at high-single-digits to low-teens easily. The last time FDS grew adj. EPS by 10%, the stock traded at 23x, for reference. But say that it only trades back to 17x (the -2 standard deviation level), I see a 70% upside, and if it goes back to the average of 25x, the stock is worth much more.

Risk

If the broader asset management operating environment stays weak and clients become more aggressive on vendor consolidation, FDS could see slower expansion, more pricing pressure, and fewer cross-sell wins even if the product is good. I call this out because the bull case now leans more on workflow expansion. If clients start narrowing provider count or pushing harder on pricing, the recent ASV acceleration may not repeat.

Conclusion
