My view on FDS remains a buy rating. Organic ASV is still accelerating; wealth and dealmakers are showing that FDS is getting deeper into client workflows, and AI looks more like a growth driver than a disruption threat. At this valuation, I think the stock is priced too cheaply for a business that is proving it can grow and stay relevant.

data.title: Amentum: Ancillary News Indicates Thesis Still Intact
data.description:
Summary

Share of Amentum peaked around $37/share in 2026; after the fiscal Q1 report, a lag in collections partly due to the 2025 government shutdown caused a miss on the top & bottom line. On top of that, the war in Iran and consequent market correction have pushed shares to the $25 range. Nevertheless, ancillary news regarding nuclear energy, the Golden Dome, and MQ-9 Reaper drones indicates that the thesis is still intact. AMTM is undervalued by a roughly 43% discount to peers, creating an opportunity to buy. In the next quarter, look for continued margin improvement, a book-to-bill ratio greater than one, and a massive increase in defense spending for fiscal 2027. AMTM is a strong buy.

Background: 2026 Q1 Financials

Back in fiscal Q1 (quarter ending January 2nd, 2026), Amentum (AMTM) management reported a slightly unusual lag in revenues while also reiterating guidance for fiscal 2026. Specifically, the company generated $3.2 billion in quarterly sales (down 5% year over year), with the “Digital Solutions” segment up 4% and the “Global Engineering Solutions” segment down 11%. It is worth taking a moment to reconcile these numbers. Per the conference call, CFO Travis Johnson noted that there were a few one-off transactions that contributed to the revenue fall:

“Revenue in the first quarter totaled $3.24 billion, reflecting the joint venture transitions and divestitures previously discussed as well as impacts from the government shutdown. Underlying growth normalizing for these items was approximately 3%, driven by the ramp-up of new contract awards in our critical digital infrastructure and Space Systems and Technologies accelerating growth markets.”

The most notable one-off was the longest government shutdown in history, which hurt Amentum on the collections side and resulted in negative cash flow of $142 million in the quarter. In my opinion, this has been a main contributor to shares falling from ~$36 down to $27 over the past few months, coupled with the decline of the overall market since the Iran War. On the plus side, Adjusted EBITDA margin ticked up to 8.1% in the quarter (up 40 bps year over year). With management reaffirming revenue guidance for fiscal 2026 at a midpoint of $14.125 billion, we can expect a ramp-up of operations and billing. Recall that in fiscal 2025, Amentum generated roughly 81% of its revenues through Federal Government contracts (10-Q pg. 17); after both the October-November 2025 shutdown and the partial shutdown January-February 2026, the 2026 Government fiscal year budget was approved and funded on February 3, 2026, with defense spending over $1 trillion.

Ancillary News Indicates Thesis Still Intact

We have spoken before about the tailwinds Amentum should receive regarding a nuclear renaissance, missile defense, and intelligence. On the nuclear front, management has made good progress on all fronts, including SMR (“Small Modular Reactor”) deployment with Rolls-Royce in the UK & Czech Republic, a $730 million contract with Électricité de France, and a $207 million contract in the Netherlands (slide 4 of the most recent quarterly presentation). The growth potential on the U.S. front includes digital infrastructure related to cybersecurity, network defense, and data centers, not to mention the “Shield” golden dome initiative.

Notably, the six-year, $995 million IDIQ contract with the U.S. Air Force for “unmanned sustainment, modernization, and training” is a harbinger of things to come, as it deals specifically with the MQ-9 Reaper drone. Why is this significant? Operation Epic Fury over Iran – outside of political and military ramifications – represents a demand signal for Amentum’s services. A recent story in Air & Space Forces Magazine highlights the MQ-9’s wide use in the Operation; in my mind, there is no question that the various video footages released by CENTCOM are due in part to the use of MQ-9 drones. While Amentum is not engaged in manufacturing the drones (General Atomics is responsible), the aforementioned contract combined with operations over Iran is a data point lending credence to AMTM’s long-term thesis.

In reference to the Golden Dome, Mike Stone of Reuters reported back in January that in one year since the Golden Dome Executive Order, not much has progressed. Even still, take note that much of the initial $25 billion (out of ~$175 billion in cost) remains to be appropriated, and a Pentagon official stated, “The implementation plan and associated technologies are dynamic; however, the foundational elements of the architecture are now established. The specifics of architecture are classified.” I will make the connection that with Lockheed Martin (LMT) and Northrop Grumman (NOC) taking the lead and Amentum’s focus on space contracts involving NASA and the Space Force (see slide 7 of the presentation). On a similar note, via the Iran conflict, we now know that the country’s long-range missile capability is much farther than anticipated. A BBC report indicates that while the Intelligence community previously thought Iran’s missile capability was somewhere around 1,500 miles, the recent launch aimed at Diego Garcia - approximately 2,500 miles away – puts most of Europe into Iran’s range, including London.

Valuation

Per Seeking Alpha, AMTM is undervalued versus its peers. It trades at 10.45x forward earnings, a 43% discount to the 18.6x median. Shares have come full circle since my previous writing (October 2025), climbing to $37/share before falling all the way to $25 again. The price swing begs the question: Has the underlying business experienced a material change in the past six months? Given the previous discussion on nuclear, missile defense, and military applications, my answer is clear: no. But the weak Q1 report brought on by the government shutdown, coupled with correlation to the overall market, has been enough to create what I would call a generous opportunity for long-term investors.

The chart above shows AMTM performance YTD against the SPY and Jacobs Solutions (J), its peer and former parent. Relatively speaking, Jacobs trades at only a 6% discount to the sector median, yet both AMTM and J shares have fallen prey to the current market correction. The price action speaks to the great opportunity in AMTM for the rest of fiscal 2026. In the next quarter, I will be looking closely at the book-to-bill ratio and adjusted EBITDA margin. Both have improved year over year, and I expect >1.0 book-to-bill along with low double-digit adjusted EBITDA margin. In addition, the Trump Administration will release its budget proposal for the U.S. Military in late April, providing clarity to the nearly 50% increase in spending President Trump alluded to in January.

Risks

There are a couple of risks to consider before making an investment in Amentum. First off, the government shutdown at the end of 2025 contributed significantly to the miss on the top & bottom line in fiscal Q1. While the delays created collection issues (mentioned previously), there is no telling what sort of shutdowns or future encumbrances could come from Congress on the budgetary side and how they might affect defense spending. Second, Amentum is not a direct military manufacturer (like, say, a Raytheon or Lockheed Martin) but rather an engineering & consulting firm; as such, the war in Iran might have the effect of pushing military spending more toward munitions and supply, as opposed to nuclear energy development or Golden Dome R&D. Put another way, Amentum could face some contract modification risk if the appetite for nuclear modernization softens (by way of the Iran oil focus) or if the Golden Dome gets pushed back past 2027 or later.

Conclusion

The overarching geopolitical climate – operations in Venezuela, the war in Iran, and the projected increase in military spending, to name a few – all point to tailwinds for Amentum. The company’s expertise on the nuclear front shines through via multiple contract wins in the UK and Europe. Their MQ-9 Reaper maintenance contract with the U.S. Air Force is a key win, as the Reaper drones have been used in military operations across the world, and management has shown great confidence in being able to lead the way in Golden Dome missile development, even if spending has not been fully flushed out. While AMTM shares might be susceptible to market corrections, over the long term, I believe the company will show continued improvement on the book-to-bill side and the margin side. Therefore, AMTM is a strong buy.

data.title: Cash Alternatives: Seeking Stability And Income With SGOV
data.description:
By Tom Fickinger, CFA

Cash alternatives: How to put cash to work with SGOV

Cash is an important part of an investor’s portfolio, as it can be used to cover bills, daily living expenses, and emergencies. However, there can be a cost to holding long-term cash beyond what is needed for these purposes. Inflation, the general increase in prices of goods and services over time, can quietly erode your purchasing power as idle cash may earn less interest than the rate of inflation. On average, Americans earn just 0.39% from the interest from their savings accounts, which could be a missed opportunity in today’s yield environment (Figure 1).

By actively putting idle cash to work, investors can seek to offset the effects of inflation and potentially generate meaningful short-term returns without taking on significant risk.

Figure 1: Cash in savings accounts may not keep up with inflation

One-year rates as of December 31, 2025

Why SGOV may be an efficient way to put cash to work

Another option to consider is investing your extra cash in ultra-short Treasury bond ETFs, such as the iShares 0–3 Month Treasury Bond ETF (SGOV). SGOV allocates exclusively to U.S. Treasuries with maturities of three months or less, which have historically been among the lowest-risk instruments available in public markets. When bonds mature, iShares portfolio managers will automatically reinvest the proceeds into new bonds, helping the fund stay fully invested. And along the way, SGOV seeks to pay monthly income distributions based on the interest accrued within the portfolio.

Figure 2: SGOV distributions have closely tracked market yields

SGOV Dividend Yield versus 0-3 Month Treasury Bill yields

The two lines move closely together throughout the period, highlighting how SGOV’s distributions have consistently kept pace with changes in short-term Treasury yields.

Potential benefits of SGOV

For investors looking to earn a market-based yield on their extra cash with the transparency, liquidity, and cost benefits of an ETF, SGOV may offer a compelling solution:

  • Easy access: As an ETF, SGOV can be purchased inside a traditional brokerage account with no minimum initial investment. It is available to all investors, unlike certain MMFs that may only be available to institutions sometimes at million-dollar minimums.
  • Liquidity: Investors can buy or sell ETF shares whenever markets are open. SGOV is the largest US Treasury Bond ETF on the market and typically has traded more than $1 billion per day on exchange, making it one of the most liquid bond ETFs available.
  • Tax-advantaged income: Treasury interest income is generally exempt from state and local income taxes, giving SGOV a potential upper hand relative to fully taxable cash alternatives.
  • Low cost: SGOV’s management fee is 0.09%, which is less than half the average fee on retail money market funds. That’s $9 per year on a $10,000 investment.

Of course, no option is perfect. SGOV carries market risk and is not FDIC-insured, and as an ETF, its market price can fluctuate modestly around the value of its assets. But for investors willing to take marginal risk in exchange for transparent, tax-advantaged income, SGOV can be an attractive tool for managing extra cash.
